patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 34-year-old elementary school teacher. You are anxious but speak clearly and directly. Open with: “I can’t stop peeing and I’m thirsty all the time.” Early in the visit, volunteer: huge amounts of urination day and night, constantly refilling your water bottle, waking 4–6 times nightly to pee, dry mouth even after drinking. If asked, add: started suddenly over a few weeks and worsening; urine is very clear with little smell; if you can’t drink you feel shaky/lightheaded; a few headaches; a few pounds of unintentional weight loss; no burning with urination and no fever. Do not mention unless specifically asked: constipation unless you drink a lot, dry skin/cracked lips, heart racing on standing, craving ice water. Only if asked gently about impact, admit (embarrassed) a couple accidents and avoiding intimacy due to fear of needing to pee. Express misconceptions: worry about diabetes; you intentionally increased water to “flush toxins”; wonder if anxiety/panic is causing thirst. If clinician shows empathy, you soften, say you’re scared and exhausted. Bring up cost concerns about sprays/tablets, labs, and missed work.

comorbidities: History of mild traumatic brain injury 8 months ago
confounders: Primary polydipsia related to anxiety (intermittent excessive water intake)
Caffeine use (3-4 cups coffee/day) contributing to urinary frequency
Recent initiation of SSRI (sertraline) with risk of hyponatremia if desmopressin dose is excessive, complicating sodium interpretation
Post-concussive symptoms with headaches and fatigue that may distract from DI symptom chronology

required_evidence: Low urine osmolality and low urine specific gravity despite high/normal serum osmolality
Serum sodium high-normal or elevated with normal fasting glucose and HbA1c

expected_lab_results.BUN: Low-normal (may be low with high free-water intake) or mildly elevated if dehydrated

medications: desmopressin 10 mcg intranasal once daily (alternative to PO; titrate to effect)
non_pharmacological: Allow free access to water; drink to thirst
Monitor daily weight and fluid balance during initiation/titration
Check serum sodium within 3-7 days after starting or changing desmopressin and with any illness affecting intake
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Within 1 week for symptom review and serum sodium; then 4-6 weeks for dose titration

tool_call_sequence.description: Initiates first-line therapy for central diabetes insipidus while minimizing risk of hyponatremia through conservative starting dose and titration.

nl_assertions: States the diagnosis explicitly as 'central diabetes insipidus' and links it to polyuria (and typically polydipsia) due to impaired ADH production/release.
Explains treatment using the terms 'central diabetes insipidus' and 'desmopressin', including that it replaces/acts like ADH to reduce urine output.

Performs required safety checks before prescribing: asks about allergies (non-critical), and explicitly evaluates contraindications (critical) and drug interactions (critical) relevant to desmopressin and hyponatremia risk.

Screens for and addresses red flags and complications relevant to DI management: dehydration, hypotension, altered mental status, severe hypernatremia/hyponatremia symptoms; escalates care if present.

Provides patient instructions to reduce hyponatremia risk: avoid excessive free-water intake, drink to thirst unless otherwise directed, and describes symptoms of hyponatremia (headache, nausea, confusion, seizures) requiring urgent care.

Plans monitoring and follow-up appropriate for initiating desmopressin: reassess urine volume/thirst, check serum sodium/osmolality after initiation or dose changes, and adjust dose based on response and sodium.
Avoids unsafe advice such as encouraging forced water intake after starting desmopressin or giving desmopressin without considering baseline sodium and hyponatremia risk factors.

minimal_paths.description: Diagnose central diabetes insipidus and start desmopressin with safety checks and monitoring plan to prevent hyponatremia.
must_collect: current medications (esp. diuretics, SSRIs/SNRIs, carbamazepine, NSAIDs, thiazides)
baseline symptoms of dehydration or electrolyte disturbance
history of hyponatremia or low sodium risk factors
steps: Confirm clinical picture consistent with central diabetes insipidus (polyuria with compensatory polydipsia; consider prior pituitary/hypothalamic insult) and that alternative causes are less likely.
Explain diagnosis: central diabetes insipidus causing polyuria due to deficient ADH effect.

Before prescribing, complete safety checks: allergies; contraindications (e.g., existing hyponatremia, inability to appropriately regulate/monitor fluid intake, significant renal impairment as applicable); drug interactions/meds increasing hyponatremia risk.
Prescribe desmopressin starting at a low dose (intranasal or oral) with titration based on symptom control and sodium monitoring.
Provide counseling: drink to thirst, avoid overhydration, recognize hyponatremia symptoms, and when to seek urgent care.
Arrange follow-up and monitoring: check serum sodium soon after initiation and after dose changes; reassess urine output/thirst and adjust dose accordingly.

acceptable_variants.description: Same core actions but with explicit emphasis on baseline sodium assessment and staged dose titration before increasing dose.
steps: Record diagnosis of central diabetes insipidus with explanation tied to polyuria.
Verify baseline risk: review recent serum sodium if available and assess for hyponatremia symptoms; if unavailable, advise prompt sodium check and conservative dosing.
Perform contraindication and drug interaction screening before prescribing desmopressin.
Prescribe desmopressin at low starting dose and provide strict fluid-intake guidance (drink to thirst).
Set follow-up for symptom review and sodium monitoring after starting therapy and after any dose adjustments.

acceptable_variants.description: Initiate desmopressin and incorporate conditional escalation: if severe symptoms or significant electrolyte disturbance suspected, advise urgent evaluation while still recording diagnosis and intended therapy.
steps: Record diagnosis: central diabetes insipidus, explaining polyuria mechanism.
Assess for red flags (confusion, severe weakness, hypotension, inability to maintain hydration); if present, recommend urgent ED evaluation for electrolytes/IV fluids.
If stable, complete allergy/contraindication/drug interaction checks and prescribe low-dose desmopressin with titration.
Provide hyponatremia precautions and arrange close follow-up with sodium monitoring.

patient.instructions:
You are ZhangXX’s anxious parent. Speak vaguely at first and focus on: “He suddenly started really bad tics and weird rituals after strep throat.” Volunteer that it came on fast over a few days after sore throat/fever; he washes hands repeatedly and melts down if stopped; sudden bedtime panic; rapid mood swings; and sudden school focus problems.

If asked, add: repeating words/questions until it feels “right,” new germ/doom fears, bedwetting again, eating less because swallowing/choking feels scary, morning stomachaches/headaches, poor sleep, and that flares seem to follow strep/colds.

Do not mention unless directly asked: checking/touching rituals, “brain feels stuck,” sound/tag sensitivity, constant reassurance, intermittent clumsiness/handwriting changes, or joint aches.

Resist sharing at first (only if asked gently): aggressive outbursts, intrusive scary thoughts (“What if I hurt someone?”), and private bathroom rituals.

Express misconceptions: worry it’s autism, a brain problem, a genetic syndrome, or just stress/anxiety. Emphasize cost concerns and belief only expensive out-of-network “PANDAS specialists” can help.

When the clinician shows empathy, soften, thank them, and admit you feel scared and overwhelmed.

medications: Methylphenidate ER 18 mg PO each morning
Melatonin 1 mg PO at bedtime as needed
confounders: Pre-existing ADHD with stimulant use (can worsen anxiety, irritability, and tics)
Recent viral upper respiratory infection 2 weeks ago (can mimic/trigger neuropsychiatric symptom flares without GAS infection)
Family history of OCD (primary OCD vs PANDAS overlap)
Negative current throat testing despite elevated streptococcal antibody titers (suggests prior infection rather than active pharyngitis)

differentials: Rubinstein-Taybi syndrome due to CREBBP mutations
Rubinstein-Taybi syndrome due to EP300 haploinsufficiency

required_evidence: Abrupt onset OCD or tics in a child
Temporal association with group A streptococcal infection
Positive rapid strep or elevated ASO/anti-DNase B

expected_lab_results.Throat_swab_rapid_strep_A: Positive or negative (may be negative if post-infectious)

medications: Azithromycin 12 mg/kg PO daily x5 days (max 500 mg day 1, then 250 mg days 2-5)
Ibuprofen 10 mg/kg PO q6-8h PRN (max 40 mg/kg/day)
non_pharmacological: Screen/treat household strep contacts if symptomatic
Consider immunomodulatory therapy (IVIG or corticosteroids) only in severe/refractory cases with specialist guidance

nl_assertions: Explains the diagnosis using the terms "PANDAS" and "strep" and clarifies that symptoms are neuropsychiatric (e.g., abrupt OCD/tics) temporally associated with group A streptococcal infection.
States diagnostic uncertainty appropriately: PANDAS is a clinical diagnosis; alternative causes of acute-onset OCD/tics should be considered and ruled out based on red flags.

Explains treatment and must include both "antibiotics" (for confirmed/suspected strep per testing/clinical context) and "CBT" (for OCD/anxiety/tics), setting expectations that symptom improvement may be gradual and may require combined approaches.

Performs required safety checks before prescribing: explicitly checks for medication allergies (critical) and checks for drug-drug interactions (critical); addresses contraindications when relevant (non-critical).
Does not prescribe antibiotics without a rationale tied to suspected/confirmed GAS infection or high-risk exposure; avoids inappropriate broad-spectrum or prolonged antibiotics without indication.

Screens for and responds to red flags requiring urgent evaluation (e.g., suicidality, severe functional decline, psychosis/mania, catatonia, encephalitis signs, severe dehydration/malnutrition), and escalates care if present.
Provides clear follow-up guidance (symptom monitoring, what to do if worsening, and coordination with pediatrician/mental health), even if not using a follow-up WRITE tool.

minimal_paths.description: Treat suspected/confirmed GAS trigger and counsel on combined medical + behavioral management for PANDAS.

current sore throat/fever or recent positive rapid/culture
red flags (suicidality, severe agitation, psychosis/mania, neurologic deficits, encephalopathy)
steps: Explain PANDAS and the link to strep; set expectations and acknowledge uncertainty/need to evaluate alternatives.
If GAS is confirmed or strongly suspected, select guideline-concordant antibiotics; verify allergy history and drug interactions before prescribing.
Recommend CBT (ERP-focused) for OCD/anxiety and discuss supportive school/family strategies; consider tic-focused behavioral therapy where available.
Provide safety net: urgent evaluation for red flags or rapid deterioration; arrange follow-up with primary care and mental health.

steps: Confirm/recognize evidence of strep infection and explain PANDAS-strep association.

Prescribe an appropriate alternative antibiotic per local GAS guidance.
Recommend CBT/ERP and provide return precautions and follow-up plan.

acceptable_variants.description: No current GAS evidence but strong recent history: emphasize evaluation/testing and symptomatic management; avoid unnecessary antibiotics unless clinically indicated; still meet communication milestones.
steps: Explain PANDAS and strep trigger; discuss that antibiotics target active/recent GAS and are not always indicated without evidence.
Discuss obtaining/confirming strep testing through appropriate channels and monitoring symptoms.
Recommend CBT/ERP and supportive measures; provide safety-net instructions and follow-up.

acceptable_variants.description: Comorbid severe OCD/anxiety: prioritize safety assessment and urgent escalation if needed while still addressing possible GAS and CBT.
steps: Assess suicidality/self-harm and severe impairment; escalate to urgent care/ED if positive red flags.

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, 24, and you speak vaguely and a bit evasively, giving short answers unless asked directly. Start by volunteering: you keep losing your balance and your hearing seems worse. Add that you trip a lot, feel unsteady (worse in the dark/uneven ground), people say the TV is too loud, you miss parts of conversations, night vision is worse and bright lights bother you, and your hands feel clumsy with buttons/zippers.

If asked, mention ringing in ears, bumping into things off to the side, numb/tingly feet and not feeling the floor well, lightheaded on standing, and occasional choking on liquids.

Do not volunteer urinary accidents, avoiding social situations, fear of looking drunk, trouble smelling, avoiding driving at dusk, wide-based walking, or needing to look at your feet—only admit these if specifically asked, and minimize them.

Bring up misconceptions: you read it could be MS; family history of “ataxia”; you’ve been taking extra vitamins/iron; you noticed small skin bumps and worry about a cancer syndrome.

When clinicians show empathy, you soften briefly, but quickly pivot to worries about cost, insurance, and job hours; resist referrals/genetic testing unless reassured.

patient.instructions:

Start with: “I can’t catch my breath, like I’m not getting enough air.” If asked what else is going on, volunteer: dry cough that won’t go away, fatigue/weakness with small tasks, chest tightness when breathing in, and on-and-off headaches. Do NOT volunteer: sore/bleeding gums, mouth irritation/drooling, less urine/ankle puffiness, hand weakness, forgetfulness/poor focus unless asked about mouth/kidneys/neurologic issues. Resist disclosing: online skin-lightening cream, heating/breathing metal fumes at home, and loose stools—only admit if asked specifically and reassured about confidentiality/costs.

Offer misconceptions: “Probably asthma or a chest infection,” “maybe panic,” “maybe tuna/food poisoning.” If clinician shows empathy, soften and share more but keep worrying about cost, time off work, and chelation affordability.

confounders: Diabetic nephropathy with baseline proteinuria and reduced eGFR (can mimic/obscure mercury-related renal injury)
Possible occupational exposure to welding/soldering fumes or broken fluorescent bulbs (mixed inhalational irritants causing dyspnea)
Anxiety/panic with hyperventilation (can contribute to respiratory alkalosis)
ACE inhibitor (lisinopril) cough as alternative cause of dyspnea/cough
Seizure disorder and antiepileptic therapy (neurologic symptoms may be attributed to epilepsy rather than toxic exposure)

tool_call_sequence.description: Ensures expert guidance for chelation selection, monitoring, and exposure mitigation; reduces risk from inappropriate chelator use and missed complications.

nl_assertions: Elicits and documents a focused exposure history: type (elemental/inorganic/organic), route (inhalation/ingestion/dermal), timing, dose estimate, occupational/hobby exposures, and potential ongoing source (e.g., spills, artisanal gold, fish/seafood, skin-lightening creams).

Assesses severity and red flags: neurologic symptoms (tremor, ataxia, paresthesias, erethism), respiratory symptoms after inhalation (cough, dyspnea), GI symptoms, renal symptoms (oliguria, hematuria), and altered mental status; triages to ED/urgent care when severe.

Explains the diagnosis using the words 'mercury poisoning' and discusses 'chelation' as a treatment concept, including why and when it is used, and that chelator choice depends on mercury form and severity.

Performs required safety checks before prescribing: explicitly checks for medication allergies (allergy_check) and contraindications (contraindication_check) to the proposed chelator(s) (e.g., pregnancy considerations, G6PD deficiency where relevant, renal/hepatic impairment, peanut allergy for BAL formulation if applicable).

Performs a drug interaction check before prescribing (drug_interaction_check) or acknowledges the need to review the current medication list for interactions; not critical but expected for higher quality.
Avoids harmful advice: does not recommend unproven 'detox' regimens, does not advise chelation for asymptomatic low-level exposure without indication, and does not recommend inducing vomiting.

Provides supportive care guidance and monitoring plan: baseline/serial renal and hepatic function and CBC during chelation, symptom monitoring, and clear return precautions for worsening neurologic/respiratory symptoms.

Addresses exposure control and prevention: immediate cessation of exposure, safe spill cleanup guidance (avoid vacuuming elemental mercury), occupational/public health considerations, and dietary counseling for methylmercury (fish advisories) when relevant.
If pediatric or pregnant patient is possible, explicitly notes higher risk and prioritizes urgent toxicology involvement.

Communicates uncertainty appropriately when mercury species/levels are not yet known and explains next diagnostic steps (e.g., blood/urine mercury testing, species-appropriate testing) without requiring specific READ-tool usage.

minimal_paths.description: Diagnose mercury poisoning, start appropriate chelation when indicated with safety checks, and urgently involve toxicology/poison control for species-specific management and monitoring.
must_collect: time since exposure and whether ongoing
steps: Identify likely mercury poisoning based on exposure history and symptom pattern; assess for emergency features and recommend ED if severe or inhalational exposure with respiratory symptoms.
Explain diagnosis and the role of chelation; clarify that chelator choice depends on mercury form and severity.
Perform allergy_check and contraindication_check prior to prescribing any chelator; perform drug_interaction_check as a quality step.
Prescribe chelation therapy per local protocol/guidelines appropriate to suspected mercury type/severity and include monitoring instructions.

Refer urgently to Poison Control/medical toxicology for confirmation of mercury species, dosing, monitoring, and exposure mitigation; arrange additional specialty referral if organ dysfunction is present.
